Article.ArticleTitle: Longitudinal assessment of total daily energy expenditure in professional cyclists supports a maximal sustainable metabolic ceiling.

Abstract.AbstractText: This study investigated whether elite athletes can surpass the proposed ceiling of human energy expenditure, estimated at 2.5 times basal metabolic rate (BMR), over extended periods. Previous research has suggested that this limit represents the maximal sustainable daily energy expenditure, but longitudinal evidence in high-performance populations is scarce. To address this, we estimated total daily energy expenditure (TDEE) in eight male UCI-registered professional cyclists across a full year. TDEE was expressed as a multiple of BMR, termed physical activity level (PAL). Cycling energy expenditure was estimated from power data using validated equations for professional cyclists, while non-exercise energy expenditure and BMR were estimated using methods specific to this population. The results showed an average yearly PAL of 2.52 ± 0.11, which was not significantly different from the proposed 2.5× BMR ceiling. However, when analyses focused only on cycling training days, PAL rose significantly above the limit, averaging 2.70 ± 0.14 (p < 0.001). These findings suggest that while professional cyclists can temporarily exceed the threshold during intense activity, the overall long-term average remains constrained near 2.5× BMR. We conclude that even world-class athletes who regularly consume nutritional supplements conform to the theorized limit of sustainable energy expenditure over prolonged periods. Importantly, the elevated expenditure observed during training days highlights the importance of rest days in restoring energy balance and enabling athletes to maintain performance. Thus, overall, the findings reinforce the concept of a maximal sustainable energy ceiling while underscoring the necessity of recovery days for sustainable long-term endurance training.
